mission.title: Install and Configure Valkey
mission.description: Valkey is a high-performance distributed key-value database optimized for caching and real-time workloads. It is suitable for applications requiring fast data access and supports various data structures and plugins. Key trade-offs include the need for careful configuration and potential complexity in managing distributed instances.

steps.title: Add Valkey Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Valkey Helm repository to your local Helm setup, allowing you to install Valkey using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add valkey https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3

```

> This adds the repository for the latest release.

steps.title: Install Valkey CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Valkey to function properly in your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-crds.yaml

```

> Ensure CRDs are installed before deploying the application.

steps.title: Install Valkey using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs Valkey in a specified namespace using Helm, ensuring you are using the correct version.

```bash

helm install valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

```

> Replace 'valkey' with your desired release name.

steps.title: Create Valkey Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Valkey custom resource to define the configuration and deployment of your Valkey instance.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: my-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> This creates a Valkey instance with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: my-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify Valkey is Running
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Valkey deployment to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> Look for the status of the pods to be 'Running'.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Valkey
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Valkey deployment and associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss unless backups are made.

```bash

helm uninstall valkey --namespace valkey

kubectl delete namespace valkey

```

> This command removes the Valkey release.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Valkey
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This step will upgrade Valkey to the latest version while maintaining your configuration.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs -n1 kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> Drain the pods before upgrading.
> This command upgrades Valkey to the specified version.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get pods -n valkey -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs -n1 kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Valkey server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Valkey pods are not running or the service is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the pod status with 'kubectl get pods -n valkey' and ensure the service is correctly set up.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'CRD not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Custom Resource Definitions were not installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using 'kubectl apply -f https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-crds.yaml'.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually happens due to misconfiguration or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n valkey' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources allocated for Valkey.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster or adjust the resource requests/limits in the Valkey configuration.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new data structures and improved performance for existing commands.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for upgrading from 8.x.

resolution.summary: You should see the Valkey pods running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n valkey' showing status 'Running'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: my-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
